intro_text:
Maho Beach is a short crescent of white sand on the Dutch side of Saint Martin, in the territory of Sint Maarten, pressed against the western threshold of Princess Juliana International Airport (IATA SXM, ICAO TNCM). A single two-lane public road, Beacon Hill Road, and a chain-link fence separate the beach from the runway. Aircraft approaching from the sea line up on Runway 10 and descend a standard 3-degree glide slope; because the threshold sits metres from the waterline at an airport elevated just 4 metres above sea level, jets cross the sand 10 to 20 metres overhead. That single number is why Maho exists as a destination.

The runway is 2,300 metres long (7,546 feet), designated 10/28, renumbered from 09/27 in 2008 to track magnetic drift. KLM ran a Boeing 747-400 from Amsterdam until the type's last scheduled appearance here on 28 October 2016, after which the route moved to the Airbus A330. The first commercial flight landed on 3 December 1943, when the field was still a wartime airstrip, and Queen Juliana of the Netherlands, for whom it is named, visited in 1944. In 2015 the airport moved 1,829,543 passengers.

There is no vegetation behind the sand to speak of. Jet blast and storm surge scour the beach, and the dune line that would normally hold it together has been stripped repeatedly. The sand comes and goes: Hurricane Irma flattened it in 2017, and as recently as August 2025 swell from Hurricane Erin scraped the beach back to rock before longshore drift returned the sand. What people picture as a permanent stage is an unstable ledge the sea rebuilds and erases on its own schedule.

The crowd organises itself around the airport schedule and the Sunset Bar and Grill, which posts arrival and departure times on boards and over a speaker. People wade in, hold cameras overhead, and brace against the noise as a turbofan passes at landing thrust a few storeys up. The other ritual happens at the fence at the departure end: tourists grip the chain-link to feel the jet blast of a plane spooling up for takeoff. The airport authority has bolted warning signs to that fence for years. They are not decorative.

Maho is one of a small set of places where commercial aviation is staged as spectacle rather than hidden behind a terminal. It is loud, genuinely dangerous at the fence, and sits on an island whose economy was nearly severed when Irma destroyed the terminal in September 2017. Everything that makes the beach famous, the proximity, the noise, the blast, is also the thing that has killed and injured people here.
spike_explainer:
The singular fact about Maho Beach is a measurement, not a mood. Aircraft on final approach to Runway 10 cross the sand at roughly 10 to 20 metres above the heads of people standing on the beach. To understand why that number is so low, and why it is structurally impossible to make it higher without closing the airport, you have to look at the geometry of the runway and the island.

Princess Juliana International Airport has one runway, 10/28, measuring 2,300 metres. For most of its length it runs parallel to the south coast of Saint Martin, and its western threshold, the Runway 10 end, terminates a stone's throw from the Caribbean. The airport sits only about 4 metres above mean sea level. Aircraft land on a conventional 3-degree glide slope, which means that for every metre of forward travel the aircraft loses about 5 centimetres of height. Trace that slope backwards from the runway threshold, across Beacon Hill Road and the perimeter fence, out over the strip of sand that is Maho Beach, and the aircraft is still only tens of metres up by the time it passes the waterline. There is no displaced threshold deep into the island that would let pilots fly the approach higher, and no room to relocate the runway, because the Simpson Bay Lagoon sits immediately inland. The sea is on one side, the lagoon on the other, and the beach is the last sliver of land before the wheels touch.

This is what separates Maho from the long list of beaches that happen to be near airports. At most airports the approach path crosses water, an unpopulated overrun, or a fenced buffer hundreds of metres deep. Here the public has free access to the exact patch of ground directly beneath short final. A wide-body's belly fills the sky; you can read the registration, the tyre treads, the open gear doors. The aircraft is close enough that the sound arrives as physical pressure, and small children and older people are routinely knocked off balance by the wash even of a landing aircraft, which is the gentler of the two events.

The takeoff end is where the danger concentrates. When a jet at the Runway 28 end (the Maho end is also used for departures when the wind favours it, but the dangerous blast at the beach comes from aircraft holding at the threshold nearest the fence and running up to takeoff thrust) spools its engines for departure, it produces a jet blast, a high-velocity, high-temperature exhaust stream, strong enough to throw an adult. Tourists deliberately grip the chain-link fence to ride this blast, a practice the local press calls fence-surfing. The airport's signs warn in plain language that jet blast and prop wash can cause extreme bodily harm and that closely approaching aircraft can result in serious injury and death. People do it anyway, because the entire culture of the beach is built on getting closer.

The consequence is not hypothetical. On 12 July 2017, around 6pm, a 57-year-old woman from New Zealand was holding the fence at the departure end as a Caribbean Airlines Boeing 737 ran up for a flight to Trinidad. The blast tore her off the fence and threw her backwards; she struck her head on concrete and died of her injuries. News reports at the time identified her only as a 57-year-old New Zealand tourist; authorities did not publicly release her name. Her death is the clearest demonstration of the spike's double edge: the same geometry that makes Maho a wonder makes the fence a place where a routine 737 departure can kill.

It is worth being precise about which aircraft do this and how often, because the spectacle is sometimes exaggerated. The biggest jets, the 747s, are gone; KLM's last scheduled 747 visit was 28 October 2016, and the Amsterdam route now flies the smaller Airbus A330. Day to day, the traffic is regional and narrow-body, with a handful of long-haul arrivals. But even an A320 or 737 on a 3-degree slope over a 4-metre-high threshold passes Maho at the same tens-of-metres height. The number does not depend on the aircraft being enormous. It depends on the runway sitting where it sits, between a lagoon and the sea, on an island with nowhere else to put it. That is why the spike is permanent: you cannot move the geometry, so the planes will always cross the sand low. The only variable is whether the people underneath understand what the signs on the fence are telling them.
honest_reckoning_note:
The thing that makes Maho famous is the thing that hurts people here, and the beach does not hide it. On 12 July 2017 a 57-year-old New Zealand woman was killed when the jet blast from a departing Caribbean Airlines Boeing 737 tore her off the perimeter fence and threw her head-first into concrete. She was doing what the warning signs explicitly forbid, gripping the fence to feel the blast, and so have thousands of others before and since. The signs on that fence are blunt: jet blast can cause extreme bodily harm and death. They are routinely treated as a prop for photos rather than a warning, and the airport authority cannot police a public beach hard enough to stop it.

The second reckoning is about the island itself, not the tourists. On 6 September 2017, eight weeks after the death at the fence, Hurricane Irma made landfall as a Category 5 storm and gutted Princess Juliana's terminal, blowing in the entrance doors, lifting the roof, and flooding the interior with rain and seawater. For an island whose economy runs on the visitors who come, in part, to watch planes land at Maho, losing the airport was an existential blow. It reopened on temporary facilities on 10 October 2017, and the full terminal reconstruction was not completed until 2024, a seven-year recovery. The low-flying planes that draw the crowd and the hurricanes that erase the beach come from the same sky.
reckoning_pullquote: The signs on the fence say jet blast can kill, and on 12 July 2017 it did.

key_facts.label: Glide slope
key_facts.value: 3 degrees, minimum 500 ft over the beach for departures

key_facts.label: Jet-blast death at the fence
key_facts.source: BBC News; NL Times; Wikipedia: Maho Beach

margin_notes.text: The 10-to-20-metre figure is for landings on Runway 10. The lethal event is a departure: the jet blast at the takeoff end, not the belly of a landing plane, is what has killed.
The airport opened as a wartime airstrip; the first commercial flight landed 3 December 1943. It is named for Queen Juliana of the Netherlands, who visited in 1944.
Maho has no stabilising dune line. Jet blast and storm surge strip it; Hurricane Erin's swell scraped it to rock in August 2025 before longshore drift rebuilt the sand.
The Sunset Bar and Grill posts the flight schedule on boards and over a speaker, and sells a shot called the Jet Blast. The crowd times itself to arrivals.
Hurricane Irma gutted the terminal on 6 September 2017. Reopened on temporary facilities 10 October 2017; full reconstruction not finished until 2024.
The fence signs warn that jet blast and approaching aircraft can cause extreme bodily harm and death. They are treated as photo props more often than warnings.

day_in_time.dawn: Early morning is quiet, before the day's bank of arrivals builds. The bars are not yet posting flights, the fence is empty, and the beach, if the sand is present that season, is just a narrow Caribbean strip beside an idle runway. Light is low and flat off the water to the west.
day_in_time.midday: Peak spectacle. Inbound traffic concentrates in the late morning and midday window as flights connect through the region, so this is when wide-body arrivals cross Maho lowest and most frequently. The crowd packs the waterline directly under the approach, cameras up, bracing for the noise.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon brings the best photographs: warm low light catches white fuselages against turquoise water, and the bar boards still list departures. Departures mean fence-surfers gather at the takeoff end, which is when the jet blast is most dangerous.
day_in_time.night: Flights thin out after dark and the spectacle ends; the beach reverts to the Sunset Bar and Grill's evening trade. Approach and runway lights pick out the occasional late arrival crossing the dark water, but the daytime crush is gone.

food_drink.name: Johnny cakes
food_drink.description: Fried or griddled cornmeal-and-flour breads, a staple across Sint Maarten and the wider Leeward Islands, eaten plain or split and filled with saltfish or cheese.
food_drink.where: Local snackettes and lolos (roadside barbecue stalls) across the Dutch side; not sold on the beach itself.

food_drink.name: Callaloo and saltfish
food_drink.description: A green leafy stew of dasheen or amaranth leaves, often served with salted cod, reflecting the island's Afro-Caribbean cooking traditions.
food_drink.where: Lolos in Philipsburg and Simpson Bay, a short drive east of Maho. Not available at the beach.

food_drink.name: The Jet Blast shot
food_drink.description: A shot named for the high-velocity exhaust from departing aircraft, served at the Sunset Bar and Grill, which built its whole identity around the runway next door.
food_drink.where: Sunset Bar and Grill, on the beach at Maho, directly facing the Runway 10 threshold.

food_drink.name: Guavaberry liqueur
food_drink.description: A local rum-based liqueur made with the wild guavaberry, traditional to Sint Maarten, especially around the December holidays; the island's old folk-song tradition is built around it.
food_drink.where: Sold in shops and bars across the island, including in Philipsburg; bring it to the beach rather than buying it there.

timeline.title: First commercial flight lands
timeline.description: The first commercial flight landed at the field on 3 December 1943, while it was still a wartime airstrip on a runway only a few hundred metres long.

timeline.title: Inaugurated as a civil airport
timeline.description: Queen Juliana of the Netherlands, for whom the airport is named, visited the island via the airport in 1944 as it transitioned to civilian use.

timeline.title: Sunset Bar and Grill built
timeline.description: Neil Rooney and a partner built the bar on Maho Beach in 1996, a year after Hurricane Luis battered the island, beside the Runway 10 threshold. It became the organising point for plane-spotting crowds.

timeline.title: Runway renumbered 10/28
timeline.description: The single runway was renumbered from 09/27 to 10/28 to reflect the gradual drift of magnetic north, the designation still in use today.

timeline.title: Last scheduled KLM 747
timeline.description: KLM's Boeing 747-400 made its last scheduled appearance at the airport on 28 October 2016, ending the era of the largest jets crossing Maho; the Amsterdam route moved to the Airbus A330.

timeline.title: Tourist killed by jet blast
timeline.description: On 12 July 2017 a 57-year-old New Zealand woman gripping the perimeter fence was thrown by the jet blast of a departing Caribbean Airlines Boeing 737 bound for Trinidad, struck her head on concrete, and died of her injuries.
timeline.source: BBC News; NL Times; Time; Wikipedia: Maho Beach

timeline.title: Hurricane Irma destroys the terminal
timeline.description: Category 5 Hurricane Irma struck on 6 September 2017, blowing in the terminal's doors, lifting the roof, and flooding the interior, crippling the island's main gateway.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Princess Juliana International Airport; ABC News

timeline.title: Reopens on temporary facilities
timeline.description: The airport resumed operations using temporary facilities on 10 October 2017, beginning a recovery that would take years to fully complete.

timeline.title: Terminal reconstruction completed
timeline.description: The full reconstruction of the terminal damaged by Irma was completed in 2024, roughly seven years after the storm, financed in part by international reconstruction funding.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Princess Juliana International Airport; World Bank

zones.name: The approach line
zones.character: The patch of sand directly under Runway 10's final approach, where landing aircraft cross 10 to 20 metres overhead. The densest crowd and the best landing photos.
zones.notes: Hold cameras tight; the wash from a landing aircraft can knock children and older people off balance even without the takeoff blast.

zones.name: The departure fence
zones.character: The chain-link perimeter fence at the runway end where departing jets run up to takeoff thrust. This is where the jet blast is strongest and where fence-surfing happens.
zones.notes: Site of the 12 July 2017 fatality. Warning signs here state jet blast can cause extreme bodily harm and death. Holding the fence during a departure is the dangerous act, not a clever photo.

zones.name: Sunset Bar and Grill frontage
zones.character: The west end of the beach in front of the bar, which posts the flight schedule. Drinks, shade, and a slightly less crushed view of the approach.
zones.best_for: Tracking the schedule and stepping back from the crowd
zones.notes: The bar's boards and speaker are the most reliable way to know what is inbound and when.

landmarks.name: Princess Juliana International Airport (SXM / TNCM)
landmarks.description: The single-runway international airport whose Runway 10 threshold sits metres from Maho Beach, forcing the famously low approaches. Its terminal was destroyed by Hurricane Irma in 2017 and rebuilt by 2024.

landmarks.name: Sunset Bar and Grill
landmarks.description: Beach bar built in 1996 directly facing the Runway 10 threshold; it posts arrival and departure times and serves a shot named the Jet Blast, and has become the social anchor of the plane-spotting scene.

cultural_refs.title: Plane-spotting and aviation photography destination
cultural_refs.description: Maho is one of the most photographed plane-spotting sites in the world; images of jets crossing the sand at landing height are a fixture of aviation media and travel coverage.

cultural_refs.title: Fence-surfing
cultural_refs.description: The local name for the practice of gripping the airport perimeter fence to feel the jet blast of a departing aircraft, the activity that has caused serious injuries and the 2017 death.

cultural_refs.title: The Jet Blast shot
cultural_refs.creator: Sunset Bar and Grill
cultural_refs.description: A signature shot at the Sunset Bar and Grill named after the runway exhaust, an example of how the beach's bars have commercialised the airport spectacle.

recurring_events.name: Daily wide-body arrival window
recurring_events.when_text: Late morning to midday, daily, schedule-dependent
recurring_events.description: The concentration of inbound flights connecting through the region pulls the largest aircraft and the biggest crowds to the approach line in a midday window each day; the Sunset Bar posts the times.

businesses.name: Sunset Bar and Grill
businesses.description: Beach bar on Maho built in 1996 by Neil Rooney, facing the Runway 10 threshold. Posts the flight schedule on boards and over a speaker, and serves a shot called the Jet Blast.
businesses.address: Beacon Hill Road, Maho, Sint Maarten

things_to_know.label: The fence kills, the approach merely buffets
things_to_know.note: Standing under a landing plane is loud and can knock you over; gripping the fence during a departure is what has caused serious injury and the 2017 death. Heed the warning signs and stay off the fence.

things_to_know.label: Time your visit to the schedule
things_to_know.note: There is nothing to see when nothing is flying. Check the Sunset Bar boards or a flight tracker; the busiest arrival window is late morning to midday, and the biggest jets are rare since the 747s left in 2016.

things_to_know.label: The beach may not be there
things_to_know.note: Maho has no stabilising dunes and is regularly stripped by jet blast and storm surge. After hurricanes the sand can be scraped to rock, as in 2017 and again in August 2025, before longshore drift rebuilds it.

things_to_know.label: Eat and drink off the beach
things_to_know.note: Food on the beach itself is limited to the bar; the island's real cooking, johnny cakes, callaloo, lolo barbecue, is a short drive away in Simpson Bay and Philipsburg.

access_note: Maho Beach is on the Dutch side of Saint Martin, immediately west of Princess Juliana International Airport (SXM), reached in a few minutes by car or taxi from the airport terminal or about 15 to 20 minutes from Philipsburg. It sits on Beacon Hill Road, the same road that runs between the beach and the runway; the Sunset Bar and Grill marks the spot. There is no fee, but parking is limited and the area is busiest in the midday arrival window.
